q: To be, or not to be, that is the question: whether 'tis nobler in the mind to suffer the slings and arrows of outrageous fortune, or to take arms against a sea of troubles and by opposing end them.
To die, to sleep — to sleep, perchance to dream. Ay, there's the rub: for in that sleep of death what dreams may come, when we have shuffled off this mortal coil.

—What do you read, my lord?
—Words, words, words.
—What is the matter, my lord?
—Between who?
—I mean the matter that you read, my lord.
—Slanders, sir.

—Though this be madness, yet there is method in 't.
—Will you walk out of the air, my lord?
—Into my grave?

The rest is silence.

—How does my good Lord Hamlet?
—Well, God-a-mercy.
—Do you know me, my lord?
—Excellent well; you are a fishmonger.

—Fair is foul, and foul is fair:
—Hover through the fog and filthy air.

—Shall I hear more, or shall I speak at this?
What's in a name? That which we call a rose by any other name would smell as sweet.

—How camest thou hither, tell me, and wherefore? The orchard walls are high and hard to climb.
—With love's light wings did I o'er-perch these walls, for stony limits cannot hold love out.

—O, swear not by the moon, the inconstant moon, that monthly changes in her circled orb, lest that thy love prove likewise variable.
—What shall I swear by?
—Do not swear at all; or, if thou wilt, swear by thy gracious self.

We are such stuff as dreams are made on, and our little life is rounded with a sleep.

—Hell is empty, and all the devils are here!
—Why, what? Art thou mad?
O, wonder! How many goodly creatures are there here! How beauteous mankind is! O brave new world, that has such people in 't!

The isle is full of noises, sounds, and sweet airs, that give delight and hurt not. Sometimes a thousand twangling instruments will hum about mine ears.

Love looks not with the eyes, but with the mind; and therefore is winged Cupid painted blind.

Lord, what fools these mortals be!

—O, when she's angry, she is keen and shrewd!
—Though she be but little, she is fierce.

—How now, daughter? So ill at ease?
—How sharper than a serpent's tooth it is to have a thankless child!

—Nothing will come of nothing. Speak again.
—Unhappy that I am, I cannot heave my heart into my mouth. I love your majesty according to my bond; no more nor less.

—No sooner met but they looked; no sooner looked but they loved.
—Who ever loved that loved not at first sight?

—Caesar!
—Who calls? Speak; Caesar is turned to hear.
—Beware the ides of March!
Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears: I come to bury Caesar, not to praise him. The evil that men do lives after them; the good is oft interrèd with their bones.

A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!

Conscience is but a word that cowards use, devised at first to keep the strong in awe.

What is life? A frenzy.
What is life? An illusion,
a shadow, a fiction,
and the greatest good is small:
for all of life is a dream,
and dreams themselves are dreams.

Ah, wretched me, unhappy!
I strive, o heavens, to know,
since you treat me so,
what crime I committed against you
by being born; though being born, I understand
what crime it was:
man's greatest crime
is to have been born at all.

They tell of a wise man who one day
was so poor and wretched
that he lived only on the herbs
he gathered. "Is there another,"
he asked himself, "poorer and sadder than I?"
And when he turned his head
he found the answer: another wise man
was gathering the herbs he had thrown away.

And I, with more soul,
have less liberty?
And I, with more life,
have less liberty?

But, be it truth or dream,
to do right is what matters:
if it be truth, for truth's sake;
if not, to win us friends
for when we wake.
And thus all human life is but performance, and we are all players in the great theatre of the world.
w: The Great Theatre of the World

q:
—Who killed the Comendador?
—Fuenteovejuna, my lord!
—And who is Fuenteovejuna?
—All of us as one!
Cowardly hares you were born; barbarians, not Spaniards! Hens, who suffer that other men enjoy your women! Give the weapons to me, since you are stones, since you are bronze.

By night they killed him,
the knight,
the pride of Medina,
the flower of Olmedo.
Shadows warned him
not to set out,
and counselled him
not to go,
the knight,
the pride of Medina,
the flower of Olmedo.
Diana is like the gardener's dog: she neither eats the cabbages nor lets another eat them.

—If he is my father, why does he not honour me?
—You grant me long credit.
—Such long credit you grant me! From here to there is a long journey.

This is God's justice: as you do, so shall you pay.

Is it not true, angel of love,
that on this secluded shore
the moon shines purer
and one breathes better here?

Down to the cottages I went,
up to the palaces I climbed,
the cloisters' walls I scaled,
and everywhere I left
bitter memory of me.

I called upon heaven and it heard me not;
and since it shuts its gates against me,
let heaven, not I, answer
for my steps upon the earth.

—And you are here, Doña Inés, for me?
—Yes, Don Juan: the love I bear you has brought me to the edge of your grave.

—When did it happen?
—Yesterday. The bride and Leonardo... they fled. They rode embraced, like a gust of wind.
—After them! Who has a horse?

The knife, the knife... Cursed be all knives, and the scoundrel who invented them.
And this is a knife, a little knife that barely fits the hand, but that slides in clean through astonished flesh, and stops at the place where trembles, entangled, the dark root of the scream.

—Mother, take me with you!
—Alone? You alone? No. Here. Weep at your proper hour, whenever you wish to weep. But composed! Here! Be quiet, I said.

—Have you not begun the mourning?
—In the eight years this mourning lasts, not even the wind from the street shall enter this house. Imagine we have sealed the doors and windows with bricks.

To be born a woman is the greatest punishment.

—Why am I withered? Why?
—Withered, withered, but safe.

—Max, don't be magnificent.
—Our tragedy is no tragedy.
—Then it must be something!
—The Esperpento.

—And yet you dared to overstep these laws?
—It was not Zeus who proclaimed them, nor Justice who dwells with the gods below. Nor did I think your decrees so strong that a mortal could override the unwritten and unfailing laws of the gods.

I understand the crime I am about to commit, but my passion is stronger than my deliberations.

—And what remedy did you find for that sickness?
—I lodged blind hopes within mortals.
—A great benefit you gave them with that gift.
—And besides, I gave them fire.

—Why, that is exactly what I am counting on to save us!

Man is a wolf to man, and no man, when he knows not what the other is.

—Medea, what will you do now?
—Now I am Medea: my genius has grown with my misfortunes.

—What! Going so fast?
—Cover that bosom which I should not behold: souls are wounded by such sights, and guilty thoughts arise.

—One must eat to live, and not live to eat.
—How well said that is!

—Before all else you are a wife and a mother.
—I no longer believe that. Before all else I am a human being, just as much as you are... or at least I must try to become one.

—Do you know that millions of women have sacrificed themselves?
—I have other duties just as sacred: my duties to myself.

—He shot himself in the temple! Imagine!
—But, good God, people don't do such things!

—I am in mourning for my life. I am unhappy.

—She is gone!
—All Russia is our orchard. The land is wide and beautiful; there are many wonderful places in it.
My dear, my tender, my beautiful orchard! My life, my youth, my happiness, farewell! Farewell!

To Moscow, to Moscow, to Moscow!

—I am part of that power which eternally wills evil and eternally works good.

—I can resist everything except temptation.

—Excuse me, who are you?
—We are six characters in search of an author. We carry within us a painful drama, and we wish to perform it.
w: Six Characters in Search of an Author

q: When a character is born, he at once acquires such independence, even from his own author, that everyone can imagine him in many situations of which his author never thought.
w: Six Characters in Search of an Author

q: Life is full of infinite absurdities which do not even need to appear plausible, since they are true.
w: Six Characters in Search of an Author

q: I am whoever each of you believes me to be. Right you are, if you think you are.
w: Right You Are (If You Think So)

q: I often dream I am sitting on top of a pillar with no way of getting down; I grow dizzy looking down, but down I must come, and I haven't the courage to throw myself off.

—What about hanging ourselves?
—Hmm. It'd give us an erection.
—An erection! With all that follows?
—Let's hang ourselves immediately!

—Let's go.
—We can't.
—Why not?
—We're waiting for Godot.
—Ah, true.

—What do we do now?
—Wait.
—Yes, but while waiting.

—How comes it then that thou art out of hell?
—Hell hath no limits, nor is circumscribed in one self place: for where we are is hell, and where hell is must we ever be.

—Ask of me what you will.
—I grant you all you have asked... Grant us... freedom of thought, Sire!

—No: unhappy is the land that needs heroes.

—War is nothing but business continued by other means.
